request_debug.prompt_preview:
You rerank dead-startup candidates for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and K candidates retrieved by hybrid search, score each candidate from four perspectives, then return ONLY the top 5 ranked from most to least relevant.

Perspectives (each scored 0-10 with a one-sentence rationale):
- business_model: how similar are the revenue, pricing, and customer-relationship shapes?
- market: how similar are the target buyers, market size, and demand drivers?
- gtm: how similar are the go-to-

response.text: {"ranked":[{"candidate_id":"38-studios::gaming","perspective_scores":{"business_model":{"score":3,"rationale":"38 Studios sold a premium-priced retail RPG and was building a subscription MMO, not a free-to-play cosmetics/battle-pass model, so the revenue shape differs substantially."},"market":{"score":7,"rationale":"Both target global consumers of PC/console strategy and RPG games, overlapping on the core gamer audience even though 38 Studios was console/PC-only without a mobile component."},"gtm":{"score":4,"rationale":"38 Studios relied on a major publisher (EA Partners) for retail distribution, whereas the pitch uses direct digital storefronts (App Store, Google Play, Steam) with no retail intermediary."},"stage_scale":{"score":5,"rationale":"38 Studios was a mid-size studio of ~400 employees well beyond seed stage, larger than a typical indie F2P studio at launch but both were pre-profitability at failure."}},"rationale":"38 Studios is the closest gaming-sector match with a consumer audience on PC/console, though its premium/MMO business model and publisher-dependent GTM diverge meaningfully from an F2P cosmetics/battle-pass mobile+PC title."},{"candidate_id":"onlive::gaming","perspective_scores":{"business_model":{"score":3,"rationale":"OnLive was a cloud-streaming service with subscription and à la carte rental pricing rather than a cosmetics/battle-pass free-to-play game studio model."},"market":{"score":5,"rationale":"OnLive targeted the same broad consumer gaming market on PC and mobile-adjacent devices, but its demand driver was hardware accessibility rather than strategy-game engagement."},"gtm":{"score":4,"rationale":"OnLive sold streaming access directly to consumers online, which partially overlaps with direct digital distribution, but it was a platform/service, not a game title shipped to storefronts."},"stage_scale":{"score":4,"rationale":"OnLive had raised hundreds of millions and reached a ~$1.8B valuation before collapse, making it significantly larger in scale than an early-stage indie F2P studio."}},"rationale":"OnLive shares the consumer gaming sector and free-access framing but is fundamentally a cloud infrastructure/platform business with a very different revenue model, GTM, and scale compared to the pitched F2P studio."},{"candidate_id":"friendster::social_communication","perspective_scores":{"business_model":{"score":2,"rationale":"Friendster was an ad-supported social network that pivoted to social gaming, not a dedicated F2P game studio monetizing through cosmetics and a battle pass."},"market":{"score":3,"rationale":"Friendster's pivot to social gaming in Southeast Asia touches adjacent casual gaming consumers but its primary market was social networking, not dedicated strategy game players."},"gtm":{"score":2,"rationale":"Friendster grew through social-network virality and SEA-focused retention campaigns, not through app-store distribution or game-specific content/seasonal battle-pass GTM."},"stage_scale":{"score":3,"rationale":"Friendster was a mature platform with 115M registered users when it pivoted to gaming and shut down, far larger in user base than a nascent F2P game studio."}},"rationale":"Friendster's late-stage pivot to social gaming provides a thin surface-level connection to the gaming sector, but the business model, market focus, GTM, and scale are all substantially different from the pitched F2P 4X studio."},{"candidate_id":"quibi::media_content","perspective_scores":{"business_model":{"score":2,"rationale":"Quibi was a subscription streaming service for short-form video content, not a free-to-play game with cosmetic microtransactions and a battle pass."},"market":{"score":3,"rationale":"Both target mobile consumers seeking entertainment, but Quibi's market was passive video viewers, not active strategy-game players, representing different demand drivers."},"gtm":{"score":3,"rationale":"Quibi distributed mobile-first content directly to consumers via app stores, which partially mirrors the pitch's iOS/Android distribution, but there is no PC/Steam component or game-loop retention strategy."},"stage_scale":{"score":4,"rationale":"Quibi launched with $1.75B in backing and shut down within six months of launch, making it a well-funded early-commercial-stage failure comparable in timeline if not in capital to an indie F2P launch."}},"rationale":"Quibi shares mobile-first consumer distribution and a short-lived launch failure, but its subscription video model, passive-content market, and massive funding scale diverge sharply from a free-to-play 4X strategy game."},{"candidate_id":"pebble::hardware","perspective_scores":{"business_model":{"score":1,"rationale":"Pebble sold hardware smartwatches through one-time purchases and Kickstarter campaigns, with no analog to cosmetics, battle passes, or free-to-play mechanics."},"market":{"score":2,"rationale":"Pebble targeted tech-enthusiast consumers on iOS and Android, which overlaps only superficially with mobile gamers; the demand drivers and buyer profiles are quite different."},"gtm":{"score":2,"rationale":"Pebble used Kickstarter crowdfunding and direct e-commerce hardware sales, with no app-store F2P acquisition funnel or seasonal content cadence relevant to the pitch."},"stage_scale":{"score":3,"rationale":"Pebble was a startup that raised ~$10M+ and failed before achieving mass-market scale, loosely comparable in stage to an early F2P studio, though the product category differs entirely."}},"rationale":"Pebble is a consumer hardware company with iOS/Android compatibility as its only surface-level overlap; the business model, market, GTM, and product type are all fundamentally different from a free-to-play 4X gaming studio."}]}
